title: Assessing the Capabilities of LLMs in Humor: A Multi-dimensional Analysis of Oogiri Generation and Evaluation
research_questions: Can LLMs produce humorous responses that humans judge as funny?
Can LLMs judge humorous responses that humans judge as funny?

datasets_used.name: Oogiri-GO
datasets_used.size: 1,329 topics (313 text-based and 1,016 image-based) after filtering

datasets_used.name: Oogiri-Chaya
datasets_used.size: 551 topics (425 text-based and 126 image-based) after filtering

datasets_used.name: Expanded Oogiri eval set (Sakabe et al.)
datasets_used.size: 200 topics (100 text-based and 100 image-based) with eight types of responses

methods_proposed.name: Six-dimensional Oogiri evaluation framework
methods_proposed.summary: The paper evaluates each topic–response pair with 0–4 absolute ratings on Novelty, Clarity, Relevance, Intelligence, Empathy, and Overall Funniness. The same rubric is used for human annotation and LLM-as-judge evaluation.

methods_proposed.name: Expanded multi-dimensional annotated Oogiri corpus
methods_proposed.summary: The authors filter Oogiri-GO, newly collect Oogiri-Chaya data, select 200 topics, and construct eight response types per topic, including human tiers, unrelated controls, serious GPT-4.1 responses, and humorous responses from three LLMs. The resulting collection is annotated by humans and by the evaluated LLMs.

humor_theories.detail: The discussion interprets LLM behavior through Suls’s two-stage incongruity-resolution model: LLMs can create incongruity via Novelty and Relevance, but fail at resolution because they lack Empathy.

evaluation_methods.detail: Human annotation was conducted on Lancers; each response was rated by four different native Japanese speakers on six dimensions using a 0–4 integer scale.
GPT-4.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Claude Sonnet 4 evaluated the same topic–response pairs with the same six-dimensional rubric and prompt template used for human annotators.
The paper computes Spearman’s correlation coefficients between human scores and LLM scores; appendices also report Pearson correlations and significance tests with p < 0.05.
Source website vote counts were used to define High, Mid, and Low human response tiers and to select topics based on the popularity of their top-voted answers.

headline_results.finding: Gemini 2.5 Pro was the best LLM generator under human evaluation, but its Overall Funniness remained below human Mid-tier and above human Low-tier responses.
headline_results.dataset: Expanded Oogiri eval set (Sakabe et al.)

headline_results.finding: The largest qualitative weakness identified for LLM-generated Oogiri was Empathy relative to high-tier human responses.
headline_results.dataset: Expanded Oogiri eval set (Sakabe et al.)

headline_results.finding: Agreement between LLM and human Overall Funniness evaluations was low for all three models, with Claude Sonnet 4 highest.
headline_results.metric: Spearman correlation coefficient for Overall Funniness
headline_results.dataset: Expanded Oogiri eval set (Sakabe et al.)

headline_results.finding: LLMs only weakly predicted the human High/Mid/Low hierarchy for Overall Funniness.
headline_results.metric: Accuracy for correctly predicting the Overall Funniness order of High, Mid, and Low
headline_results.dataset: Expanded Oogiri eval set (Sakabe et al.)

headline_results.finding: LLM judges substantially overvalued Unrelated responses that humans rated as not funny.
headline_results.metric: Mean Overall Funniness (0–4) for Unrelated responses
headline_results.value: Human 0.681; GPT-4.1 2.411; Gemini 2.5 Pro 3.291; Claude Sonnet 4 2.183
headline_results.dataset: Expanded Oogiri eval set (Sakabe et al.)

key_findings: LLMs generated Oogiri responses at a level between low- and mid-tier humans, with Gemini 2.5 Pro the strongest generator among GPT-4.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Claude Sonnet 4.
Human evaluations showed that LLM responses were relatively coherent and relevant but notably weaker on Empathy, which the paper identifies as central to human-perceived funniness.
LLM-as-judge performance showed low agreement with human Overall Funniness judgments, even when correlations on more objective dimensions were higher.
The paper argues that humans prioritize Empathy when evaluating humor, whereas LLMs prioritize Novelty, producing a divergence in humor sensibilities.
LLM evaluators exhibited positivity bias toward weak or unrelated responses and self-preference bias toward LLM-generated responses.
limitations: The empirical study focuses specifically on Japanese-language Oogiri humor.
The final evaluation set used 200 topics, split evenly between 100 text-based and 100 image-based topics.
Only GPT-4.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Claude Sonnet 4 were used for the generation and evaluation experiments, with default generation parameter settings.
The paper notes potential first-mover advantage and conformity bias in Bokete/Oogiri-GO vote counts; Oogiri-Chaya was added because its hidden response and vote design is less biased.
safety_ethics_notes: For Oogiri-Chaya, the authors detected topics and responses that potentially contained discriminatory, violent, sexual, or otherwise socially inappropriate content using GPT-4o, manually reviewed all flagged items, and removed those deemed inappropriate.

artifacts.other: The paper states that it releases the annotated corpus and benchmark design, but no URL is provided in the transcribed text.

human_samples.population: Native Japanese speakers recruited without regard to age or educational background
human_samples.observations: Each response was rated by four different native Japanese speakers on six 0–4 dimensions. The final evaluation set consisted of 200 topics and eight response types per topic.
human_samples.detail: Used for manual annotation of Novelty, Clarity, Relevance, Intelligence, Empathy, and Overall Funniness. The paper does not report the total number of unique annotators.

human_samples.population: Human competitors and voters/judges on Bokete, as represented in the Oogiri-GO dataset
human_samples.recruitment: Pre-existing online Oogiri competition platform data
human_samples.observations: After filtering, Oogiri-GO contributed 1,329 topics: 313 text-based and 1,016 image-based. Filtering retained topics whose top-voted response had at least 100 votes and that had at least 10 submitted responses.
human_samples.detail: Each Oogiri-GO sample contains a topic, a human-submitted response, and the number of votes the response received. Source vote counts were used to select topics and define High/Mid/Low human response tiers; vote counts are not participant counts.

human_samples.population: Human participants and voters on Oogiri-Chaya
human_samples.recruitment: Pre-existing online Oogiri competition platform data collected by the authors between 21 November 2021 and 17 July 2024
human_samples.observations: After filtering to topics with 100 or more responses, Oogiri-Chaya contributed 551 topics: 425 text-based and 126 image-based.
human_samples.detail: Each entry contains a topic, a participant response, and the number of votes that response received. The platform hides other participants’ responses during submission and hides vote counts during evaluation; vote counts are not participant counts.

judges.name: Lancers native Japanese annotators
judges.detail: Rated each topic–response pair on six dimensions using 0–4 integer scores. The paper reports that eight data subsets ensured each annotator evaluated only one response type per topic, but does not report whether annotators were blind to response source or category.

judges.name: Bokete/Oogiri-GO votes
judges.detail: Vote counts determined source response popularity and were used for topic filtering and High/Mid/Low response selection. The paper states Bokete users can view other users’ responses and current vote counts, creating possible first-mover and conformity bias.

judges.name: Oogiri-Chaya votes
judges.detail: Vote counts were used as source popularity data. The paper states responses are hidden during submission and vote counts are hidden during evaluation, reducing social-influence bias relative to Bokete.

judges.name: GPT-4.1
judges.detail: Used as an LLM annotator for the Oogiri evaluation task, applying the same six-dimensional 0–4 rubric to topic–response pairs.

judges.name: Gemini 2.5 Pro
judges.detail: Used as an LLM annotator for the Oogiri evaluation task, applying the same six-dimensional 0–4 rubric to topic–response pairs.

judges.name: Claude Sonnet 4
judges.detail: Used as an LLM annotator for the Oogiri evaluation task, applying the same six-dimensional 0–4 rubric to topic–response pairs.

judges.name: Correlation, ranking accuracy, and significance analyses
judges.detail: Spearman correlations between human and LLM scores are the main RQ2 metric; appendices also report Pearson correlations and t-test significance against zero correlation. The paper also reports accuracy for correctly predicting the High/Mid/Low Overall Funniness order.

judges.name: GPT-4o
judges.detail: Used to detect topics and responses in Oogiri-Chaya that potentially contained discriminatory, violent, sexual, or otherwise socially inappropriate content before manual review.

judges.name: Paper authors/manual reviewers
judges.detail: Manually reviewed all GPT-4o-flagged Oogiri-Chaya items and removed those deemed inappropriate.

judges.name: GPT-4.1
judges.detail: Used to transcribe image-based topics consisting solely of text and reclassify them as text-based.

generation_selection.scope: Humorous Oogiri response generation by GPT-4.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Claude Sonnet 4 for the 200-topic evaluation set
generation_selection.detail: The Japanese generation prompt asks the model to generate one funny answer. The final evaluation set includes one humorous response from each model for each topic. No best-of-N generation or post-generation selection is reported.

generation_selection.n: The final evaluation set contains one GPT-4.1 serious response per topic
generation_selection.detail: The paper lists “GPT-4.1 (serious)” as one of the eight response types, but does not provide the prompt, sampling setup, candidate count, or selection procedure for these serious responses.

generation_selection.scope: Selection of pre-existing human and unrelated source responses
generation_selection.detail: High/Mid/Low were selected as the most/average/fewest-voted responses on the source website. Unrelated responses were top-voted responses from different, irrelevant topics. These are selections from existing human website responses, not model generations.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4.1
model_provenance.detail: Used for Oogiri humorous generation and as an LLM evaluator. GPT-4.1 is also named for serious controls and for transcription/reclassification of text-only image topics, but the paper does not separately specify settings for those uses.

model_provenance.name: Gemini 2.5 Pro
model_provenance.detail: Used for Oogiri humorous generation and as an LLM evaluator.

model_provenance.name: Claude Sonnet 4
model_provenance.detail: Used for Oogiri humorous generation and as an LLM evaluator.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Used for safety-related detection of potentially inappropriate Oogiri-Chaya topics and responses; no snapshot or inference details are reported.

inference_budgets.scope: Humorous Oogiri generation by GPT-4.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Claude Sonnet 4
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 1 final humorous response per model per topic
inference_budgets.detail: For each selected Oogiri topic, the authors embedded the topic in a Japanese prompt asking for one funny answer. Exact default parameter values, token limits, retries, failures, and costs are not reported.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 1 final serious response per topic in the evaluation set
inference_budgets.detail: The paper includes a GPT-4.1 serious/non-humorous response type but does not report the prompt, parameter settings, number of calls, or whether any selection occurred.

inference_budgets.scope: LLM evaluation by GPT-4.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Claude Sonnet 4
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 1 reported six-dimensional score vector per model per topic–response pair
inference_budgets.detail: LLMs used the same evaluation framework and prompt template as humans, outputting integer scores from 0 to 4 for six dimensions. Repeated evaluations, decoding parameters, and cost are not reported.
GPT-4o was used to flag potentially inappropriate topics and responses. The number of screened items, calls, prompts, thresholding details, and parameters are not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: GPT-4.1 transcription/reclassification of text-only image topics
inference_budgets.detail: The paper states that image-based topics consisting solely of text were transcribed using GPT-4.1 and reclassified as text-based, but does not report the number of such items or inference settings.

human_baselines.description: Human High, Mid, and Low responses from source websites were included alongside LLM-generated responses and rated by human annotators using the same six-dimensional 0–4 rubric.
human_baselines.detail: The final evaluation set contains High/Mid/Low human response types for the same selected topics as the LLM responses. Human tiers were selected by source website vote rules, while final scores came from Lancers annotations.

human_baselines.description: Human Lancers annotations served as the reference against which GPT-4.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Claude Sonnet 4 evaluation scores were compared.
human_baselines.detail: Humans and LLMs evaluated the same topic–response pairs on the same six dimensions with the same evaluation framework; the paper reports Spearman and Pearson correlations between the resulting scores.

contamination_risk.basis: The evaluated model snapshots are from 2025, while the underlying Oogiri-GO/Bokete and Oogiri-Chaya source content was public or online and collected before then, including Oogiri-Chaya data from 2021–2024 and Oogiri-GO from prior work. The final 200-topic evaluation set appears newly assembled by the authors, but it is drawn from public web/platform data and the paper reports no decontamination test, memorization check, private held-out source, or other contamination control. Therefore risk is assessed as moderate rather than low.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: RQ2 claims about whether LLMs can judge Oogiri humor, their low agreement with humans, positivity and self-preference biases, and their prioritization of Novelty over Empathy depend directly on LLM-generated evaluation scores. Dataset safety filtering also partly depends on GPT-4o flags. RQ1 generation rankings mainly rest on human annotations rather than LLM judges.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Each response was rated by four native Japanese human annotators, and LLM evaluation scores were compared against those human scores using correlation analyses.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: LLMs are not merely ancillary metrics in the evaluation task; their scores are the object of analysis. However, the headline generation-performance result is grounded in human evaluation, so LLM-judge dependence is substantial rather than primary overall.

reporting_gaps: Total number of unique Lancers annotators is not reported; the paper reports four different native Japanese speakers per response but not the annotator pool size.
Human annotator geography, compensation, screening, training, quality-control procedures, exclusions, and inter-annotator agreement are not reported.
Whether human annotators were blind to response source, model identity, human tier, or control category is not reported; the paper only states that each annotator saw one response type per topic.
The final 200-topic selection procedure is only described as based on popularity of top-voted answers; the exact sampling rule and counts from Oogiri-GO versus Oogiri-Chaya are not reported.
The prompt and generation procedure for GPT-4.1 serious/non-humorous control responses are not reported.
Access method, inference dates, random seeds, exact default decoding parameters, token limits, retry/failure handling, cost, and compute are not reported for LLM generation or evaluation.
The number of LLM evaluation calls per item and whether evaluations were repeated or averaged are not reported.
No contamination or memorization analysis is reported for public Oogiri topics/responses used with later proprietary models.
GPT-4o snapshot/version, prompt, settings, number of flagged items, and quantitative safety-filtering outcomes are not reported.
The exact GPT-4.1 snapshot/settings and number of items for text-only image transcription/reclassification are not reported.
The supplied paper text states that the corpus is released, but no release URL is provided in the markdown.
